common_name: Honorine Jobert Japanese Anemone (Classic White Fall Anemone)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; Japanese anemones spread by underground stolons to form expanding colonies — plant where they have room to naturalize or where their spreading can be managed.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; Honorine Jobert is one of the most beloved fall-blooming perennials in the partial-shade garden — a classic single white Japanese anemone that has been in continuous cultivation since 1858 and is considered the benchmark cultivar for its species. The large, single flowers (2.5–3.5 inches across) are pure white with a slight pink flush on the outside of the petals and a prominent central boss of bright-yellow stamens — a clean, elegant, luminous combination that lights up the partial-shade border in September and October when few other plants are in full bloom. The long bloom season (6–8 weeks) and the timing — bridging the gap between the main summer perennial season and frost — make Honorine Jobert uniquely valuable in the shade garden. Sturdy, 3–4 foot stems rise above the dark-green, deeply lobed foliage and branch repeatedly, producing many flowers per stem over the extended season. Japanese anemones spread by underground stolons to form expanding colonies — a spreading habit that is a practical advantage in naturalizing shade garden beds but requires management to prevent encroachment on neighbors. Divide in spring when clumps become crowded (every 4–6 years) to maintain vigor. In zone 9, Japanese anemones thrive in the partial shade of tall pines or on the north side of structures where summer heat is moderated.

common_name: September Charm Japanese Anemone (Clear Pink Fall Anemone)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; plant in spring for establishment before the summer heat; newly transplanted Japanese anemones may be slow to establish but become vigorous spreaders once settled.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; September Charm is one of the finest pink-flowered Japanese anemone cultivars — producing clear, medium-pink, single flowers with a slightly deeper pink exterior and prominent yellow stamens on stems somewhat shorter and more compact than Honorine Jobert. The clear, unmuddied pink color (neither the pale blush of some cultivars nor the strong cerise of others) is one of September Charm's most consistently praised qualities: the pink is bright, cheerful, and long-lasting over the 6–8 week fall bloom season. The individual flowers are slightly smaller than Honorine Jobert (2–2.5 inches) but are produced in similar abundance on well-branched stems. September Charm typically begins blooming in late August in zone 7–8 — one to two weeks earlier than many other Japanese anemone cultivars — then continues through October. The earlier bloom start extends the display season significantly. Like all Japanese anemones, September Charm spreads by underground stolons and benefits from spring division every 4–6 years. The dark-green, deeply lobed foliage is attractive from spring through hard frost. Pair September Charm with Honorine Jobert for a contrasting white-and-pink combination in the fall shade garden — the two cultivars bloom simultaneously and create a complementary display.

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in fall or early spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; Soft Caress is significantly more shade-tolerant than most other mahonia species and thrives in the deep shade under tree canopies where few other shrubs perform.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; Soft Caress is a revolutionary mahonia introduction from China — the first mahonia to produce completely thornless, soft, fine-textured leaves that can be touched without the painful spines that characterize traditional mahonias like Mahonia bealei and M. aquifolium. The soft, narrow, willow-like leaflets are arranged in the typical pinnate mahonia pattern but are smooth-edged and as soft to the touch as fine grass — making Soft Caress the first mahonia that can be used in foot-traffic adjacent plantings, children's play areas, and along pathways without hazard. The graceful, arching form (3–5 feet tall, similar spread) with cascading, fine-textured leaflets creates an exceptionally refined and unusual texture in the shade garden — unlike any other widely available shade shrub. In fall and early winter (October–December), Soft Caress produces racemes of bright-yellow flowers along the stem tips, followed by small, blue-black, powdery-coated fruits that are attractive to birds. The plant thrives in deep shade where few other shrubs grow well — performing under dense tree canopies that exclude even azaleas and camellias. Cold-hardy to the low-single-digits Fahrenheit, it is reliably perennial throughout zone 7–9. A landmark introduction for shade gardeners.

planting.note: Plant container plants or divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Elegans develops its best glaucous blue-gray color and largest leaf size in partial to full shade — direct sun bleaches the blue tones to yellow-green and scorches the large leaves.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Elegans is one of the most magnificent large-leaved hostas ever selected — a cultivar of Hosta sieboldiana (the blue siebold hosta) that produces exceptionally large (up to 18 inches long, 14 inches wide), heavily corrugated, intensely glaucous blue-gray leaves arranged in a massive, layered, bowl-shaped mound. The intense blue-gray color, the deeply puckered and corrugated leaf surface that catches shadows and creates dramatic texture, and the sheer scale of a mature plant (2–3 feet tall, 4–6 feet wide after several years) make Elegans one of the most impressive foliage plants available for the shade garden — a bold focal point that commands attention from any distance. In summer, pale lavender-white flowers appear on sturdy scapes above the foliage — modest flowers, but a pleasant accent to the dominant foliage display. Elegans grows slowly but steadily, becoming more magnificent each year; a 10-year-old plant in ideal conditions can reach 5–6 feet in diameter. In zone 9, hostas perform well in deep shade with consistent moisture and mulched soil — the combination of shade, moisture, and mulch moderates soil temperature enough to maintain the hosta through the Gulf South summer. Divide in early spring every 5–8 years when clumps become crowded or vigor declines.

common_name: Regal Mist Ruby Muhly Grass (Pink-Red Fall Muhly)

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to lean soil in full sun; Regal Mist develops its best and most vivid pink-red flower cloud color in full sun — in shade the fall color is significantly less intense.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to lean soil in full sun; Regal Mist is a named cultivar of Muhlenbergia capillaris (Gulf muhly grass) — a compact selection bred for improved and more intensely saturated pink-to-red flower cloud color compared to the standard species, and for a slightly more compact, upright habit that is more practical for typical residential garden spaces. In late September through November, Regal Mist produces a spectacular haze of airy, deep rose-pink to magenta-red flower plumes that rise above and envelope the entire plant in a luminous, cloud-like color display — one of the most dramatic fall ornamental effects in American horticulture. The backlit effect when the late afternoon sun shines through the flower cloud from behind is particularly spectacular, creating a glowing, luminous pink-red haze effect that is unforgettable. Muhlenbergia capillaris is a native Gulf Coast grass, naturally occurring from coastal dunes and pine savannas from Virginia to Texas — fully adapted to Gulf South heat, humidity, salt spray, sandy or clay soil, and drought. Regal Mist is exceptionally drought-tolerant once established and thrives in the lean, well-drained conditions where many ornamental grasses struggle. Plant in masses of 5 or more for maximum visual impact — the cloud effect is most dramatic when multiple plants create a sweeping pink-red haze across a broad planting area.
